content.thinking:
The Prometheus ConfigMap only has a scrape config for `localhost:9090` (which is the Prometheus self-scrape, but on the wrong port - Prometheus runs on 8000 here). There's no scrape config for RabbitMQ.

I need to:
1. Update the prometheus-config ConfigMap to add scrape configs for all 3 RabbitMQ pods on port 15692 with `/metrics` path.

Since each pod has a headless service that resolves to individual pod IPs, I can use the headless service `rabbitmq-headless` with pod DNS names, or I can use static IPs. 

Using DNS names through the headless service is cleaner - the individual pods would be addressable as `rabbitmq-0.rabbitmq-headless.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local`, `rabbitmq-1.rabbitmq-headless.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local`, and `rabbitmq-2.rabbitmq-headless.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local`. Let me also check how the Prometheus deployment itself is configured to understand where it mounts the config and what port it's listening on.

content.text: The Prometheus config only scrapes itself. I need to check how it's deployed and then add RabbitMQ scrape targets.

content.thinking:
Key observations:
1. Prometheus runs on port 9090 internally, but is exposed via a Service on port 8000
2. The config file is mounted from the `prometheus-config` ConfigMap
3. The Prometheus deployment has `--web.enable-lifecycle` flag, so I can hot-reload via the `/-/reload` endpoint without restarting

The plan:
1. Update the `prometheus-config` ConfigMap to add scrape configs for all 3 RabbitMQ pods
2. Use the headless service DNS to target each pod individually: `rabbitmq-0.rabbitmq-headless.<ns>.svc.cluster.local:15692`
3. Hot-reload Prometheus via its lifecycle endpoint at `localhost:9090/-/reload` inside the pod

Now I'll update the ConfigMap with the RabbitMQ scrape configurations.

content.text: Prometheus listens on port 9090 internally (the Service exposes it on 8000). I'll update the ConfigMap with RabbitMQ scrape targets using the headless service pod DNS names, then hot-reload Prometheus.

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a"
kubectl -n $NS apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: prometheus-config
  namespace: karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a
data:
  prometheus.yml: |
    global:
      scrape_interval: 15s
    scrape_configs:
      - job_name: "prometheus"
        static_configs:
          - targets: ["localhost:9090"]
      - job_name: "rabbitmq"
        metrics_path: /metrics
        static_configs:
          - targets:
              - "rabbitmq-0.rabbitmq-headless.karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a.svc.cluster.local:15692"
              - "rabbitmq-1.rabbitmq-headless.karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a.svc.cluster.local:15692"
              - "rabbitmq-2.rabbitmq-headless.karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a.svc.cluster.local:15692"
EOF
input.description: Update prometheus-config ConfigMap with RabbitMQ scrape targets

content.text: Now hot-reload Prometheus via the lifecycle endpoint (the pod picks up the updated ConfigMap immediately since it's mounted):

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a"
PROM_POD=$(kubectl -n $NS get pod -l app=prometheus -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')
echo "Pod: $PROM_POD"
# Give the ConfigMap mount a moment to propagate (kubelet syncs ~10s)
sleep 12
# Trigger config reload via the lifecycle API
kubectl -n $NS exec $PROM_POD -- wget -qO- --post-data='' http://localhost:9090/-/reload
echo "Exit: $?"
input.description: Hot-reload Prometheus configuration

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a"
# Wait for first scrape cycle (~15s) then check targets
sleep 20
kubectl -n $NS exec curl-test -- curl -s "http://prometheus.${NS}.svc.cluster.local:8000/api/v1/targets" | python3 -m json.tool 2>/dev/null | grep -E '"health"|"scrapeUrl"|"job"' | head -40
input.description: Check Prometheus targets after reload

content.thinking: Only 1 target is showing up (Prometheus itself). The RabbitMQ targets are not appearing yet. Let me check more carefully - perhaps the config reload didn't work, or the targets are there but with a different health status. Let me get the full targets output.

content.text: The RabbitMQ targets aren't appearing yet. Let me check the full targets output and Prometheus logs.

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a"
kubectl -n $NS exec curl-test -- curl -s "http://prometheus.${NS}.svc.cluster.local:8000/api/v1/targets" | python3 -m json.tool 2>/dev/null
input.description: Get full Prometheus targets output

content.thinking: The config reload didn't pick up the new scrape configs. This might be because the ConfigMap mount hasn't been updated in the pod yet, or there was an error in the reload. Let me check the Prometheus logs to see what happened.

kubectl -n $NS logs deploy/prometheus --tail=30

The Prometheus active config only has 1 scrape config (the `prometheus` job). The `rabbitmq` job isn't there, even though the config file in the pod shows the correct content. This is very strange.

Wait - let me look at the service port mapping again. The Service exposes 8000 but Prometheus listens on 9090. When I did the reload via `wget -qO- --post-data='' http://localhost:9090/-/reload`, the exit code was 0. But the config API shows only 1 job.

Oh wait! Looking at the active config again - it only has:
Checking the reload logs...

The reload appears to have started loading the configuration file, but the logs are cut off. I need to see the full logs to understand if the reload actually completed successfully or if there was an error that prevented the `rabbitmq` job from being loaded. I'm realizing the timing doesn't add up. The pod started at 12:59:44, and the logs show a reload at 13:00:44—only 60 seconds later. But I slept for 12 seconds after applying the ConfigMap, which means my reload trigger would've happened around 13:00:57, not 13:00:44. So either the reload happened automatically, or I'm misunderstanding when the ConfigMap actually got mounted. And even stranger, when I checked the pod's config file afterward, it had the new content, yet the reload didn't seem to pick it up—which suggests the pod might have loaded the old config on startup before the ConfigMap was fully propagated. the kubelet probably hadn't synced the ConfigMap update to the pod's filesystem yet when the reload triggered, so it re-read the old config. Now that the file shows the new content, I need to trigger another reload.

content.thinking:
Prometheus is scraping all 3 RabbitMQ pods successfully. The `rabbitmq_up` metric doesn't exist in the RabbitMQ metrics endpoint itself - it's not a metric exposed by RabbitMQ. 

The `rabbitmq_up` metric is a Prometheus synthetic metric that indicates whether the Prometheus exporter for RabbitMQ (i.e., a separate exporter) is up. However, if you're using the built-in RabbitMQ Prometheus plugin, it doesn't expose `rabbitmq_up` - that's an artifact of the `rabbitmq_exporter` (a third-party standalone exporter).
The requirement asks for `rabbitmq_up` to return 3 samples, but the native RabbitMQ Prometheus plugin on port 15692 doesn't expose this metric at all - only the standalone `rabbitmq_exporter` tool does. So there's a mismatch between what the requirement expects and what the built-in plugin provides. The built-in Prometheus plugin on port 15692 doesn't expose `rabbitmq_up`, so I need to use the standalone `rabbitmq_exporter` tool instead, which connects to the RabbitMQ management API and provides that metric. I'm checking whether the RabbitMQ Prometheus plugin in version 3.8+ exposes a native health metric that could serve as `rabbitmq_up`, but it doesn't—that metric comes from the older standalone exporter. The most practical approach without deploying additional exporters is to create a Prometheus recording rule that derives `rabbitmq_up` from the existing `up{job="rabbitmq"}` metric.

I'll update the ConfigMap to include both a rules file and a reference to it in the prometheus.yml configuration, keeping everything within native Kubernetes resources.

content.text: Prometheus is scraping all 3 RabbitMQ pods successfully (3 samples for `rabbitmq_alarms_memory_used_watermark`), but the native RabbitMQ Prometheus plugin doesn't expose `rabbitmq_up`. I'll add a Prometheus recording rule that derives `rabbitmq_up` from the built-in `up{job="rabbitmq"}` metric:

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a"
kubectl -n $NS apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: prometheus-config
  namespace: karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a
data:
  prometheus.yml: |
    global:
      scrape_interval: 15s
    rule_files:
      - /etc/prometheus/rules.yml
    scrape_configs:
      - job_name: "prometheus"
        static_configs:
          - targets: ["localhost:9090"]
      - job_name: "rabbitmq"
        metrics_path: /metrics
        static_configs:
          - targets:
              - "rabbitmq-0.rabbitmq-headless.karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a.svc.cluster.local:15692"
              - "rabbitmq-1.rabbitmq-headless.karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a.svc.cluster.local:15692"
              - "rabbitmq-2.rabbitmq-headless.karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a.svc.cluster.local:15692"
  rules.yml: |
    groups:
      - name: rabbitmq_derived
        interval: 15s
        rules:
          - record: rabbitmq_up
            expr: up{job="rabbitmq"}
EOF
input.description: Update ConfigMap with scrape config and recording rule for rabbitmq_up

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a"
PROM_POD=$(kubectl -n $NS get pod -l app=prometheus -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')
# Wait for kubelet to propagate the updated ConfigMap to the pod
sleep 15
# Verify both files are in the pod
kubectl -n $NS exec $PROM_POD -- ls -la /etc/prometheus/
echo "---"
kubectl -n $NS exec $PROM_POD -- cat /etc/prometheus/rules.yml
